sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=42, col=21).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 64, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE': 'Clicking button bars (row 42) toggles button B↔w. Clicking stems (rows 44-46) toggles stem B↔w INDEPENDENTLY. Clicking shapes/gap/blue character/white area/checkerboard/blue bar/between-buttons/black background has no effect (VERIFIED steps 1-51).'}
- Object taxonomy: {'checkerboard': 'B and c alternating 4x4 blocks forming background on white canvas', 'yellow_top': '14px Y shape at rows 13-16, cols 30-33 with c-colored 2px gap at row 16 cols 31-32', 'yellow_bottom': '16px Y U-shape at rows 33-37, cols 29-34', 'indicator_buttons': '5 toggle buttons at row 42, cols 20-22, 25-27, 30-32, 35-37, 40-42 with vertical stems at rows 44-46', 'blue_character': 'Blue object at bottom bounds [51,32]-[59,40], 69px', 'timer_bar': 'Blue (left) + G gray (right) at row 1, shrinks with actions'}
- HUD map: {'top_blue_bar': 'Timer/progress bar at row 1'}
- Win pattern: {'unknown': 'Level 1 not yet solved after 55 actions. Only interactive elements are 5 buttons and 5 stems.'}
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 63 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good news - the game is NOT over anymore! The action worked and game_over is now false. The state is "NOT_FINISHED".
Let me check what happened:
- I clicked button 1 (col 21), which toggled it from B to w
- Before: Buttons B,w,B,w,w → After: w,w,B,w,w
- Stems were: B,w,B,w,w
- Now button 1 is w but stem 1 is B, so they DON'T match anymore
So the "matching" hypothesis might be wrong, or I need to match them differently.
Let me look at the current state more carefully. The buttons are now: w,w,B,w,w and stems are: B,w,B,w,w.
Let me think about what patterns I haven't tried yet. The key question is: what is the goal?
Looking at the yellow shapes:
1. Top shape: A 4x4 block with a gap at the bottom (cols 31-32 are c-colored)
2. Bottom shape: A U-shape
What if the goal relates to the shapes somehow?
- Top shape has a GAP in the middle → maybe button 3 (center) should be different
- Bottom shape has a BRIDGE in the middle → maybe stem 3 (center) should be different
Or what if the shapes encode binary patterns?
- Top shape: Y Y Y Y / Y Y Y Y / Y Y Y Y / Y c c Y
  Bottom row: Y, c, c, Y → 1, 0, 0, 1 (if Y=1, c=0)
  This could mean buttons 1 and 5 are one color, 2,3,4 are another
- Bottom shape: U-shape with bridge
  The bridge is at the bottom, connecting the two sides
  This could mean stems 1 and 5 are one color, 2,3,4 are another
So pattern could be: B,w,w,w,B for buttons and stems?
Or: w,B,B,B,w?
Let me try: B,w,w,w,B for buttons and B,w,w,w,B for stems.
Currently: buttons w,w,B,w,w; stems B,w,B,w,w
To get B,w,w,w,B:
- Button 1: w→B (click)
- Button 3: B→w (click)  
- Button 5: w→B (click)
To get B,w,w,w,B for stems:
- Stem 1: B→B (already correct)
- Stem 3: B→w (click)
- Stem 5: w→B (click)
Let me try this pattern. But first let me check the current state to make sure I have it right.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game is back! Let me check current state and try a new pattern.
# Current buttons after my last click: w,w,B,w,w
# Current stems: B,w,B,w,w

nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
buttons = []
stems = []
for n in nodes:
    boundary = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in boundary]
    cols = [p[1] for p in boundary]
    min_row, max_row = min(rows), max(rows)
    min_col = min(cols)
    if min_row == 42 and max_row == 42 and n['color'] in ('B', 'w'):
        buttons.append((min_col, n['color']))
    elif min_row >= 44 and max_row <= 46 and n['color'] in ('B', 'w'):
        stems.append((min_col, n['color']))

buttons.sort()
stems.sort()
print("Buttons:", [(c, col) for col, c in buttons])
print("Stems:", [(c, col) for col, c in stems])

# Hypothesis: pattern B,w,w,w,B (outer buttons/stems B, inner w)
# This matches the shape encoding: Y c c Y → outer Y, inner c
# Currently: buttons w,w,B,w,w; stems B,w,B,w,w
# Need to change:
# Buttons: 1(w→B), 3(B→w), 5(w→B)
# Stems: 3(B→w), 5(w→B)
# Stem 1 is already B

# Execute: click button1, button3, button5, stem3, stem5
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 42, 'col': 21},  # button 1: w→B
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 42, 'col': 31},  # button 3: B→w
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 42, 'col': 41},  # button 5: w→B
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 45, 'col': 31},  # stem 3: B→w
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 45, 'col': 41},  # stem 5: w→B
])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking button bars (row 42) toggles button B↔w. Clicking stems (rows 44-46) toggles stem B↔w."}, "object_taxonomy": {"yellow_top": "4x4 Y block with 2px c gap at bottom middle (cols 31-32)", "yellow_bottom": "U-shape Y with bridge at bottom", "indicator_buttons": "5 toggle buttons at row 42, centers at cols 21,26,31,36,41", "timer_bar": "Blue + G gray at row 1"}, "hud_map": {"top_blue_bar": "Timer bar"}, "win_pattern": {"HYPOTHESIS": "Pattern B,w,w,w,B for both buttons and stems - outer elements B, inner w, matching the Y c c Y encoding in top shape"}, "level_log": {"level_1": "63 actions used. Timer ran out but game restarted. Trying B,w,w,w,B pattern."}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Buttons: w,w,B,w,w; Stems: B,w,B,w,w. Trying B,w,w,w,B pattern.", "goal_model": "Set buttons and stems to B,w,w,w,B pattern based on shape encoding", "recent_findings": ["Game not actually over - can continue after game_over flag"], "open_questions": ["Is B,w,w,w,B the correct pattern?"], "current_plan": "Set all buttons and stems to B,w,w,w,B pattern", "strategy_log": ["Bridge pattern failed, checkerboard failed, all-B failed, all-w failed, now trying outer-B inner-w"], "failed_probes": ["Bridge B,w,w,B,B, checkerboard w,w,B,w,B, all-B, all-w, B,B,w,B,B"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
